content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission' 
victoria E. Davis 
Deputy county Counsel 
County of Yolo 
625 Court street, Room 201 
P.O. Box 127 
Woodland, CA 
95695 
Dear Ms. Davis: 
July 27, 1988 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-88-268 
You·have requested confirmation of our telephone advice 
concerning a mass mailing by the Yolo County Clerk to 
approximately 500 people who served on elections boards in the 
precinct on election day. 
These election workers were sworn in 
and received a small amount of compensation from the county for 
their services. 
In our telephone conversation, I advised you that such a 
mailing was not prohibited by Government Code section 89001. 
Your letter accurately reflects the telephone advice I provided. 
If you have any further questions regarding this matter, 
please contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
DMG:KED:plh 
Sincerely, 
Diane M. Griffiths 
General Counsel 
. / . 
C 
.~ 
!<.atZ(/~{JV'-- L, bt-ZLc '1-tL-1.. '--
By: 
Kathryn E. Donovan 
Counsel, Legal Division 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660 


CHARLES R. MACK 
COUNTY COUNSEL 
TO: 
FROM: 
DATE: 
RE: 
County of Yolo 
625 COURT STREET, ROOM 201 
PO BOX 127 
(916) 666,8172 
~lEMORANDUM 
Kathy Donovan, Fair Political Practices Commission 
Victoria E. Davis, Deputy County Counsel 
July 13, 1988 
Proposition 73 Mass Mailing 
This memorandum is to confirm our telephone conversations of 
July 11 and 12 with regard to the Yolo County Clerk wanting to 
send letters of recognition to persons who served on elections 
boards 
in 
the 
precinct 
on 
election 
day. 
There 
are 
approximately 
500 
such 
persons, 
who 
received 
symbolic 
remuneration for a long day's work. 
Your advice to me was that the County Clerk I s contemplated 
action was permissible under Proposition 73. 
You based your 
advice in part on a similar incident in which the Governor 
desired to send thank you letters to State employees who 
performed extra work during the floods of 1986. 
The Governor 
was running for re-election at the time, so Government Code 
§89001 was 
an issue. 
The 
FPPC 
advised the Governor his 
mailing was not prohibited, in part because the recipients 
were State employees, rather than constituents at large. 
The 
election vlOrkers, having been sworn and received compensation 
from the County, are situated similarly to the State employees 
whom the Governor wished to recognize, so the County Clerk may 
send his letters. 
I 
assume 
I 
will hear from you if this memorandum does not 
accurately reflect our conversations as you understood them. 
VED/gal 


California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
victoria Davis 
Deputy County Counsel 
County of Yolo 
P.o. Box 127 
Woodland, CA 
95695 
Dear Ms. Davis: 
July 15, 1988 
Re: 
88-268 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political Reform 
Act was received on July 14, 1988 by the Fair Political 
Practices commission. 
If you have any questions about your 
advice request, you may contact Kathryn Donovan, an attorney in 
the Legal Division, directly at (916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, 
• 
unless your request poses particularly complex legal questions, 
or more information is needed, you should expect a response 
within 21 working days if your request seeks formal written 
advice. 
If more information is needed, the person assigned to 
prepare a response to your request will contact you shortly to 
advise you as to information needed. 
If your request is for 
informal assistance, we will answer it as quickly as we can. 
(See Commission Regulation 18329 (2 Cal. Code of Regs. Sec. 
18329) .) 
You also should be aware that your letter and our response 
are public records which may be disclosed to the public upon 
receipt of a proper request for disclosure. 
DMG:plh 
Very truly yours, 
,0('.. l~ )It 
Diane M. Griffiths 
General Counsel

sections.conclusion: In our telephone conversation, I advised you that such a mailing was not prohibited by Government Code section 89001. Your letter accurately reflects the telephone advice I provided.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a mass mailing by the Yolo County Clerk to approximately 500 election workers (who were sworn in and received compensation) sending letters of recognition violates Government Code section 89001 under Proposition 73.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advised that the County Clerk's proposed mailing to election workers is not prohibited by Government Code section 89001, based on the analogy to a similar situation where the Governor was permitted to send thank-you letters to State employees who performed extra work during emergencies.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains three related pieces: (1) incoming memorandum from Deputy County Counsel confirming telephone conversation, (2) FPPC acknowledgment letter dated July 15, 1988, and (3) FPPC advice letter dated July 27, 1988 confirming the advice. The final dated response is the formal advice letter from Counsel Kathryn E. Donovan.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a mass mailing by the Yolo County Clerk to approximately 500 election workers (who were sworn in and received compensation) sending letters of recognition violates Government Code section 89001 under Proposition 73.

CONCLUSION: In our telephone conversation, I advised you that such a mailing was not prohibited by Government Code section 89001. Your letter accurately reflects the telephone advice I provided.

embedding.summary: The FPPC confirmed informal telephone advice that a Yolo County Clerk's mass mailing to approximately 500 election workers sending letters of recognition is not prohibited by Government Code section 89001 under Proposition 73.
